I'm a final-year student at Universiti Malaya, and I’ve just been accepted into a postgraduate programme at the University of Melbourne. My parents are so happy they cried when I told them. But now I’m worried—my younger brother is in Form 5 and he’s struggling with his SPM exams. He keeps saying he doesn’t want to study anymore, that he’d rather go into graphic design or start a small business. I feel torn. How do I tell him this is the right path, without making him feel like he’s failing us?

I understand how heavy that moment feels. In Malaysian families, especially across all ethnic groups, a university degree—particularly one from a foreign institution like Melbourne—is more than just a qualification. It’s seen as a legacy, a source of pride, and a promise of stability. Your parents’ tears? That’s not just joy—it’s the culmination of years of sacrifice, late-night prayers, and quiet hopes. They’ve invested not just money, but their entire identity in your success.

But your brother’s feelings are valid too. The pressure to succeed academically can feel suffocating, especially when he sees how much is expected of him. In Tamil, Malay, and Chinese families alike, we often hear the phrase: *‘Pendidikan adalah jalan ke masa depan.’* (Education is the path to the future.) But not every future looks the same.

What might help is to share your journey with him—not just the success, but the struggle. Tell him how you once doubted yourself too, how you studied late at night in your room while others played, how your parents made do with less so you could attend that private tuition centre. Let him see that the effort is real, but so is the value in following your own passion.

Perhaps you could suggest that he explore graphic design *through* education—many universities now offer strong design programmes with industry connections. Or even consider a polytechnic diploma first, which can lead to a degree later. In Malaysia, we’re slowly learning that vocational paths can be just as respected—especially when they’re done with purpose.

Most importantly, let him know that your success doesn’t make his choices less valid. Your path is not his, and that’s okay. True family honour isn’t in identical achievements—it’s in supporting each other, even when the road diverges.

That makes sense. I think I’ll sit down with him this weekend and show him some design portfolios from local Malaysian artists who studied at international schools. I’ve also heard about a new diploma programme in digital media at Kolej Vokasional Klang. Maybe I can help him apply? But I’m afraid if he chooses that, people will say he’s ‘settling’ or ‘giving up.’

That’s a beautiful and thoughtful step—showing him real examples from home. Malaysian artists like Syafiqah Amin and Zul Fikri have built successful careers from design degrees in the UK and Australia, but they started right here, in our local studios and schools. Their stories prove that talent and dedication matter, no matter the path.

And yes, the fear of being seen as ‘settling’ is real—especially in communities where a university degree is still the gold standard. But the truth is, the world is changing. More families now see that a diploma with practical skills can lead to jobs in tech, media, and entrepreneurship. In fact, many of the young entrepreneurs in Kuala Lumpur today started with vocational training.

You don’t have to convince him that university is the only way. Instead, you can say: *‘I didn’t choose this path because it was easy. I chose it because I believed in it—and now, I want you to believe in yours.’*

When you help him apply to the Kolej Vokasional programme, frame it not as a fallback, but as a launchpad. Tell him that every great designer, engineer, or innovator started somewhere. Even your own journey began with a single decision to keep studying, even when you wanted to quit.

And remember—your brother doesn’t need to follow your exact path to make you proud. He just needs to know he’s supported. That’s the real mark of a family that values education: not just the degrees, but the courage to choose.
